sentences.base_language: I wake up in the morning and wash my face.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜아서/어서 (aseo/eoseo) connects two actions where the first leads naturally into the second: 일어나서 세수해요 (wake up and wash face). Unlike 〜고 (and), 〜아서/어서 implies sequence and sometimes cause — the first action enables the second.

이 (i) = teeth (native Korean). The Sino-Korean synonym is 치아 (chia), more common in formal or medical contexts. 닦다 (dakda) = to scrub/wipe — used for teeth, shoes, and floors. Not the same as 씻다 (ssitda) = to wash with water.

머리를 감다 (meoireul gamda) is the specific verb for washing hair — not 씻다. Similarly, 세수하다 = wash face, 샤워하다 = shower. Korean has distinct verbs for different washing actions, rather than a single 'wash' for everything.

K-beauty (케이뷰티) refers to Korea's multi-step skincare routines and beauty industry, globally influential since the 2010s. The 10-step skincare routine — toner, essence, serum, sheet mask, and more — originated in Korea.

입다 (ipda) = to wear / put on (clothing). Its counterpart is 벗다 (beotda) = to take off. Korean uses different verbs for wearing different items: 신다 (shoes), 쓰다 (hat/glasses), 끼다 (gloves/ring), 매다 (tie). 입다 is for clothing worn on the body.

다 (da) = 'all / completely': 다 됐어요 (all done), 다 먹었어요 (finished eating). 준비가 됐다 (junbiga dwaetda) = 'to be ready' — literally 'preparation has become [complete]'. The passive-feeling 되다 (doeda) is very common in Korean for states that come about.
